1.headline: The amendment strips out hearings, ads and waiting time from the revocable consent process but keeps outdoor dining inside it.
1.delivers: It attacks the delays witnesses complained about: the mandatory public hearing before a revocable consent is approved becomes optional at the agency's discretion, City Record notice drops from 15 days to six and the requirement that the applicant buy repeat ads in a daily and a weekly newspaper -- the roughly $800 print notice Laura Shepard singled out -- is deleted outright. It also lets a consent take effect before the comptroller finishes registering the agreement and says an inadvertent missed notice no longer voids an approval, both of which remove common sources of dead time.
1.falls_short: It does not do the central thing Open Plans, the Hospitality Alliance and others asked for: outdoor dining stays a revocable consent rather than moving to a Department of Transportation permit or license, and the amendment in fact routes sidewalk cafes back through the charter's general revocable consent chapter instead of the administrative code sections that now govern Dining Out NYC. Nothing here merges the separate sidewalk and roadway applications, makes the program year-round or caps or eliminates the consent fees themselves -- only the newspaper ad cost goes away.
1.goes_beyond: The changes apply to every revocable consent the city grants, not just restaurant seating, so sheds, pipes, vaults and other private uses of the sidewalk also lose the guaranteed hearing and the 15-day notice; community boards, borough presidents and council members can also sign blanket waivers giving up notice for whole categories of consents, and the mayor gains authority to hand off charter powers over these agreements to agency heads.
1.notes: Two things worth a second read. First, the change to Section 364(e): the deleted text pointed sidewalk cafes to administrative code 19-160 through 19-160.6; the new text points them to "this chapter." I read that as sidewalk cafes remaining revocable consents and being folded into the general charter process, which is close to the opposite of the "get outdoor dining out of revocable consent" ask -- worth confirming against the commission's own explanation. Second, the newspaper ad requirement sits inside a long bracketed deletion in Section 371(d), so confirm the brackets really do encompass the whole daily/weekly notice passage. Also note Council Member Lincoln Restler is counted among the backers of this idea but actually testified against the charter route, preferring pending council legislation; that is a dissent, not support. Effective date is March 1, 2027 and pending petitions are grandfathered under the old rules.

2.headline: The amendment centralizes procurement data under one director and loosens public-notice rules, but skips the timelines and outcome measures witnesses wanted.
2.delivers: It gives the city's chief procurement officer, who runs the Mayor's Office of Contract Services, formal charter control over the citywide contract data system, including its format and policy, which echoes calls to give that office real authority and to standardize contract information. It lets the Procurement Policy Board scale reporting requirements by contract size, industry or small-business status, close to the ask that paperwork be sized to the grant so small community groups can compete, and it requires the board to meet at least quarterly. It also unlocks a bottleneck by allowing the mayor's sign-off on certain contracts to be handed down to an agency's chief contracting officer, with that agency head's consent, where the charter previously barred any such delegation.
2.falls_short: Nothing in the text sets the prescriptive step-by-step procurement deadlines, or penalties for agencies that miss them, that witnesses repeatedly demanded, and nothing addresses invoicing, payment or the years-long registration delays nonprofits described. It does not require cost-effectiveness or value-for-money review before large contracts, does not mandate outcome measurement or a public annual report on procurement, does not expand the Procurement Policy Board to 10 members as the Independent Budget Office director proposed and does nothing to build the single, cross-agency view of vendors that would let the city spot duplicate invoices or aggregate buying.
2.goes_beyond: It rolls back existing public input that no witness asked to cut: agencies would no longer publish a draft plan for client-services contracting or hold the annual public hearing on it, and the fixed $100,000 threshold that triggers public notice and comment on noncompetitive contracts would be replaced by whatever dollar figure the Procurement Policy Board sets by rule, with the comment period allowed to shrink to seven days and materially identical renewals up to $10 million exempt entirely. Those notice changes would not take effect until Oct. 1, 2030.
2.notes: Two things to verify before publication. First, the file appears incomplete: it jumps from Section 6 straight to Section 10, so Sections 7 through 9 are missing and may contain material provisions. Second, the delete/add markers are inconsistent — brackets appear in the Sections 325 and 326 passages but not in the Section 311 passage, so I inferred that the five-member board composition is existing law and the quarterly-meeting requirement is new; that should be checked against the current charter. Also confirm the current statutory comment period in Section 326 (the amendment sets a floor of seven days) to say precisely whether that is a cut and by how much. The flagged_ideas file's own ballot note says the amendment "requires public notice and comment before certain contract awards"; that requirement already exists, and this amendment mainly relaxes it, so that summary line should be corrected.

3.headline: It loosens two brakes on street redesigns but skips speed cameras, enforcement powers and deadlines, and adds unrelated real estate rules.
3.delivers: The amendment goes at two of the specific delays witnesses named. It rewrites the city's "major transportation projects" law so that when the Transportation Department consults another agency, that agency's head is the only person who has to be consulted, which ends the practice of individual firehouses signing off on bike lane and bus lane plans, and it says a failure to consult does not void the project. It also writes a broad definition of the public safety exception in that law: if the commissioner finds a risk of serious injury based on crash rates or any other safety consideration, the project qualifies for immediate implementation and the required council and community board notice and presentation can be made afterward on a best efforts basis.
3.falls_short: Nothing here touches automated enforcement, so the requests for more speed cameras and for the city to stop asking Albany for permission go unanswered, and Ira Gershenhorn's specific ask for cameras on the Henry Hudson Parkway is outside what a charter amendment can grant. It also does not move traffic enforcement from the Police Department back to Transportation, does not set any deadline for firehouse or agency comment, and does not impose delivery timelines on the Design and Construction Department. The underlying notice and presentation requirements for ordinary projects stay in place; only the safety exception widens, and how much that speeds up a typical redesign depends on how aggressively the commissioner invokes it.
3.goes_beyond: Most of the text has nothing to do with streets: it repeals the charter section governing city acquisition of office space and replaces it with a notice-only procedure, exempts city land sales under 10,000 square feet and sales of landmark development rights from full land use review, and lets the mayor sell undevelopable slivers of city land directly to neighboring owners at appraised value without an auction starting Dec. 31, 2030. No street safety witness asked for any of that.
3.notes: Two things to verify before publication. First, the street safety provisions work by reference to administrative code section 19-101.2, the 2018 major transportation projects law, which is not reproduced in the ballot text; an editor should read that section to confirm exactly which notice and presentation steps the widened exception lets DOT postpone, and confirm my characterization that the firehouse conferral requirement lives there. Second, the bracketed deletions in sections 6 and 7 also strip a requirement for a fresh hearing with 45 days' City Record notice when two years pass between land use approval and execution of a deal — worth checking whether that matters for any pending disposition. Also confirm the effective date split: the street safety and office space changes take effect on certification, while the no-auction sliver sales provision is delayed to Dec. 31, 2030, which is unusual and worth asking the commission about.

4.headline: The amendment orders a shared permit-filing portal by 2029 and moves waterfront permits to Buildings, but leaves each agency's permits intact.
4.delivers: It takes a real step toward the "one front door" witnesses described: a new section 645(e) requires the Department of Buildings, the Department of Environmental Protection, the Fire Department, the Department of Transportation and other permit-issuing agencies to build and accept applications through a single construction-permit system, to be established by Dec. 1, 2029. It also does one true consolidation, stripping the "exclusive" permitting, inspection and zoning-enforcement powers over wharves, piers, docks and bulkheads from the agency that runs the waterfront (Charter section 1301) and handing construction and demolition on waterfront property to Buildings under a new section 643(8), with existing permits and certificates carried over so no one has to refile.
4.falls_short: It does not make Buildings the single permitting and inspection authority that witnesses asked for: the Fire Department, environmental protection and transportation keep issuing their own permits and running their own inspections, and nothing in the text requires one application, one inspection sequence or all objections delivered at once. The shared system is also softer than it looks, since it is "subject to appropriations" and the Buildings commissioner can push the 2029 deadline back to whatever later date he or she "determines may be feasible," with no penalty for missing it. Nothing addresses the other asks: no rulemaking power for Buildings to update the building code without a Council local law, no change to permit fees or violation penalties, no multilingual small-business licensing portal and nothing on events, outdoor dining or business improvement district permits, since the new system covers new construction only.
4.goes_beyond: The waterfront transfer, which is the amendment's biggest substantive change, was not something any of these witnesses raised, and it is delayed until Dec. 31, 2030 or until a waterfront construction code exists, whichever comes first. The amendment also rewrites Buildings' internal management in ways no testimony asked for, letting the commissioner deputize additional architect or engineer deputy commissioners and replacing the five borough superintendents with borough commissioners who can be given citywide powers.
4.notes: Two things worth a second look. First, the bracket convention: section 1301(c) is replaced entirely by the word "Reserved," which I read as repealing the waterfront agency's exclusive enforcement and permit-fee power rather than merely trimming it — worth confirming against the official ballot text. Second, the effective-date clause in section 11 splits the amendment in two: the waterfront transfer and section 643.1 wait until 2030 or a new waterfront code, while the portal mandate and the deputy/borough commissioner changes take effect on certification of the vote. Also flag that section 643.1 refers interchangeably to "certificates of completion" and "certificates of occupancy," which looks like sloppy drafting and may confuse readers. One factual note: the transferring agency is identified in the Charter by function (public markets, wharf and waterfront property, airports), and the ideas file attributes those functions to Small Business Services — confirm the current agency name before publishing.

5.headline: The amendment writes rainy day fund rules into the charter but sets a lower target and leaves the formula to City Hall.
5.delivers: It moves the Revenue Stabilization Fund from a purely optional savings account into the charter with a numeric target: reserves of at least 12 percent of the prior year's tax revenue, with the city required to deposit money whenever it falls below that line. It requires the Office of Management and Budget, consulting the comptroller, to publish a deposit methodology by May 1, 2027, after a City Record notice, a 30-day comment period and a public hearing, with copies to the Council speaker, the Independent Budget Office, the state comptroller and the Financial Control Board, refreshed at least every four years. It also puts the first limit on raiding the fund: taking more than half of it in a year requires the mayor to certify a compelling fiscal need such as a recession, falling revenue, a disaster or a declared emergency.
5.falls_short: The target is 12 percent, not the roughly 16 percent proponents such as the Citizens Budget Commission urged, and it counts other existing reserve funds toward the total rather than requiring that much in the rainy day fund itself. The deposit mandate is only as binding as the methodology the mayor's own budget office writes, the amount is left to that document and no deposit is required until the fiscal year beginning July 1, 2027. Withdrawals are not actually restricted to recessions and emergencies: up to 50 percent of the fund can be drawn in any year through an ordinary budget modification for any reason, and above that the mayor certifies the need himself with no outside sign-off. Nothing in the text addresses the honest multi-year budget baselines that Council Member Wong and others paired with the fund rules, and there is no recurring public report on the fund's balance.
5.goes_beyond: Letting other city reserve funds count toward the 12 percent target was not something witnesses asked for and it reduces how much new saving the mandate actually forces. The mayoral certification path for large withdrawals also creates an escape hatch broader than the recession-and-emergency trigger proponents described, which partly answers the economists who warned against tying the city's hands.
5.notes: The 16 percent figure comes from the idea summary, not from a verbatim witness quote in the file, so confirm the Citizens Budget Commission's stated target before printing it. The provided text of subdivision 2 ends with a comma rather than a period, suggesting the excerpt may be truncated or contain a transcription error; check the certified ballot text for any missing clause. Also worth checking against the existing Section 1528 as added by Local Law 215 of 2019 to confirm which words are additions, since the source file carries no brackets or underlining. Effective date is immediate upon certification of the Nov. 3, 2026 vote.
